title: Probabilistic Generative Modeling Notes
text:
1 Preface
These are my notes on probabilistic generative modeling.
The goal is to organize ideas around diffusion models, normalizing flows, inverse problems, and applications in imaging, audio, and neuroscience.
This is not meant to be a finished textbook yet. It is a living set of research notes.

title: 2  Basics
text:
2.1 Change of Variables
In probability theory, when a random variable is transformed by a function its density changes and we can compute the new density using the change-of-variables formula. Let’s consider a simple example to illustrate this concept. Suppose we have a random variable \(X\) with density \(p_X(x)\), and we define a new random variable \(Y\) as a scaled version of \(X\):
\[
Y = g(X) = sX,
\] where \(X,Y \in \mathbb{R}^d\) and \(s \in \mathbb{R}\).
Using the change-of-variables formula, we can write the density of \(Y\) in terms of the density of \(X\) as follows: \[
p_Y(y)
=
p_X \left(g^{-1}(y)\right)
\left|
\det \frac{\partial g^{-1}(y)}{\partial y}
\right|.
\tag{2.1}\]
For the specific transformation \(Y = sX\), the inverse function is given by \[
g^{-1}(y) = \frac{y}{s},
\] and the Jacobian of the inverse transformation is \[
\left|\frac{\partial g^{-1}(y)}{\partial y}\right|
=
\left|\frac{1}{s} I\right| = |s|^{-d}.
\]
Hence, \[
p_Y(y)
=
|s|^{-d}
p_X\!\left(\frac{y}{s}\right).
\]

title: 2  Basics
text:
2.1.1 Gaussian Example
Suppose \(X \sim p_X(x)\) is a Gaussian random variable with mean \(\mu\) and covariance \(\sigma^2 I\), i.e., \(p_X(x) = \mathcal{N}(x;\mu,\sigma^2 I)\) and we define \(Y = sX\) where \(s\) is a positive scalar. Using Equation 2.1 We can compute the density of \(Y\) using the change-of-variables formula as follows: \[
p_Y(y)
=
s^{-d}
p_X\left(\frac{y}{s}\right)=
s^{-d}
\mathcal{N}\left(\frac{y}{s};\mu,\sigma^2 I\right).
\]
Expanding this expression, \[
p_Y(y)
=
s^{-d}
\frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}\sigma^d}
\exp\left(
-\frac{\left\|\frac{y}{s}-\mu\right\|^2}{2\sigma^2}
\right).
\]
Since \[
\left\|\frac{y}{s}-\mu\right\|^2
=
\frac{\|y-s\mu\|^2}{s^2},
\] we get \[
p_Y(y)
=
\frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}(s\sigma)^d}
\exp\left(
-\frac{\|y-s\mu\|^2}{2s^2\sigma^2}
\right).
\]
Therefore, \[
Y \sim \mathcal{N}(s\mu,s^2\sigma^2 I).
\]

title: 3  DDPM
text:
3.1 Motivation
Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models generate samples by reversing a gradual noising process.

title: 3  DDPM
text:
3.3 VP-SDE
Consider the VP-SDE
\[
d x_t
=
-\frac{1}{2}\beta(t)x_t\,dt
+
\sqrt{\beta(t)}\,dW_t.
\]
Its perturbation kernel is
\[
p(x_t \mid x_0)
=
\mathcal{N}
\!\left(
x_t;
s(t)x_0,
\lambda(t)^2 I
\right),
\]
where
\[
s(t)
=
\exp\!\left(
-\frac{1}{2}
\int_0^t \beta(\tau)\,d\tau
\right),
\]
and
\[
\lambda(t)
=
\sqrt{
1-
\exp\!\left(
-\int_0^t \beta(\tau)\,d\tau
\right)
}.
\]
…





Ho, Jonathan, Ajay Jain, and Pieter Abbeel. 2020. Denoising
Diffusion Probabilistic
Models. arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2006.11239.


Karras, Tero, Miika Aittala, Timo Aila, and Samuli Laine. 2022.
Elucidating the Design Space of
Diffusion-Based Generative
Models. arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2206.00364.

title: 4  EDM
text:
4.1 Probability Flow ODE
Diffusion models involve several design choices. The first is the forward noising process, which defines the perturbation kernel \(p(x_t \mid x_0)\) and includes formulations such as DDPM, VP-SDE, and VE-SDE. The second is the model parameterization, where the neural network may predict the score, noise \(\epsilon\), or clean data \(x_0\). The third is the sampling algorithm used to generate samples from the learned distribution, such as ancestral sampling, DDIM, or probability flow ODE solvers.
Karras et al. (2022) argued that we can use the same probability flow ODE for diffusion models trained using different design choices. Specifically, they claimed that the VE-SDE, corresponding to the perturbation kernel \(p(y_t \mid y_0) = \mathcal{N}(y_t; y_0, \sigma^2(t)I)\) with \(\sigma(t) = t\), where \(y_0 \in \mathbb{R}^d\) represents samples from the data distribution \(p_\text{data}\) and \(y_t \in \mathbb{R}^d\) is the corresponding noise-perturbed sample, induces probability paths that are less curved. This results in reduced discretization error and enables faster and more accurate sampling.
The reverse probability flow ODE corresponding to the perturbation kernel above is given by
\[
\mathrm{d}y = -\dot{\sigma}(t)\sigma(t)\nabla_y \log p(y;\sigma(t))\mathrm{d}t.
\]
Using the relationship given by Tweedie’s formula,
\[
\nabla_y \log p(y;\sigma(t))
=
\frac{D(y;\sigma(t))-y}{\sigma(t)^2},
\]
the probability flow ODE can be written in terms of the denoised sample \(D(y;\sigma)\) as
\[
\mathrm{d}y
=
-\dot{\sigma}(t)\sigma(t)
\frac{D(y;\sigma(t))-y}{\sigma(t)^2}
\mathrm{d}t.
\]

title: 4  EDM
section: 4.2 Sampling a VP-SDE Model with the VE-SDE ODE
text:
4.2 Sampling a VP-SDE Model with the VE-SDE ODE
Suppose the diffusion model was trained using the VP-SDE process defined by the perturbation kernel
\[
p(x_t \mid x_0) = \mathcal{N}(x_t;s(t)x_0,\lambda(t)^2I),
\]
where \(x_0 \in \mathbb{R}^d\) is a sample from the data distribution \(p_\text{data}\).
To relate the VP-SDE formulation to the VE-SDE probability flow ODE, define
\[
y_t = \frac{x_t}{s(t)}.
\]
Then \(y_t\) follows the perturbation kernel
\[
p(y_t \mid y_0) = \mathcal{N}(y_t;y_0,\sigma^2(t)I),
\]
with \(x_0=y_0\), meaning that both processes are initialized from the same data sample.

4.2.1 Relationship between \(p_{x_t}(x)\) and \(p_{y_t}(y)\)
The marginal density of \(x_t\) is
\[
p_{x_t}(x) = \int p(x_t \mid x_0)p_{\mathrm{data}}(x_0)dx_0.
\]
Substituting the VP-SDE perturbation kernel,
\[
p_{x_t}(x) = \int p_{\mathrm{data}}(x_0)\mathcal{N}(x_t;s(t)x_0,\lambda^2(t)I)dx_0.
\]
Using
\[
\mathcal{N}(x_t;s(t)x_0,\lambda^2(t)I)
=
s(t)^{-d}\mathcal{N}\left(\frac{x_t}{s(t)};x_0,\frac{\lambda^2(t)}{s^2(t)}I\right),
\]
and defining
\[
\sigma(t)=\frac{\lambda(t)}{s(t)},
\qquad
y_t=\frac{x_t}{s(t)},
\]
we obtain
\[
p_{x_t}(x)
=
s(t)^{-d}\int p_{\mathrm{data}}(x_0)\mathcal{N}(y_t;x_0,\sigma^2(t)I)dx_0
=
s(t)^{-d}p_{y_t}(y).
\]


4.2.2 Relationship between \(\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{x_t}(x)\) and \(\nabla_{y_t}\log p_{y_t}(y)\)
Using
\[
p_{x_t}(x)=s(t)^{-d}p_{y_t}(y),
\]
we have
\[
\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{x_t}(x)
=
\nabla_{x_t}\log\left(s(t)^{-d}p_{y_t}(y)\right)
=
\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{y_t}(y).
\]
Since \(y_t=x_t/s(t)\),
\[
\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{y_t}(y)
=
\frac{1}{s(t)}
\nabla_{y_t}\log p_{y_t}(y).
\]
Therefore,
\[
\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{x_t}(x)
=
\frac{1}{s(t)}
\nabla_{y_t}\log p_{y_t}(y),
\]
or equivalently,
\[
\nabla_{y_t}\log p_{y_t}(y)
=
s(t)\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{x_t}(x).
\]


4.2.3 Denoiser Expression for \(\epsilon\) Parameterization
Suppose the model is trained for \(\epsilon\) prediction, where the network is conditioned on the discrete time index and is denoted by \(\epsilon_\theta(x_t,(M-1)t)\).
The score of \(p_{x_t}\) is given by
\[
\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{x_t}(x)
=
-\frac{1}{\lambda(t)}
\epsilon_\theta(x_t,(M-1)t).
\]
Using the relationship derived above,
\[
\nabla_{y_t}\log p_{y_t}(y)
=
-\frac{s(t)}{\lambda(t)}
\epsilon_\theta(x_t,(M-1)t).
\]
Since \(\sigma(t)=\lambda(t)/s(t)\),
\[
\nabla_{y_t}\log p_{y_t}(y)
=
-\frac{1}{\sigma(t)}
\epsilon_\theta(x_t,(M-1)t).
\]
Using Tweedie’s formula,
\[
D(y;\sigma)
=
y-\sigma\epsilon_\theta(x_t,(M-1)t).
\]
Substituting \(x_t=s(t)y\) gives
\[
D(y;\sigma)
=
y-\sigma\epsilon_\theta(s(t)y,(M-1)t).
\]





Karras, Tero, Miika Aittala, Timo Aila, and Samuli Laine. 2022. Elucidating the Design Space of Diffusion-Based Generative Models. arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2206.00364.
